paper.title: Probing for Knowledge Attribution in Large Language Models
paper.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) often generate fluent but unfounded claims, or hallucinations, which fall into two types: (i) faithfulness violations - misusing user context - and (ii) factuality violations - errors from internal knowledge. Proper mitigation depends on knowing whether a model's answer is based on the prompt or its internal weights. This work focuses on the problem of contributive attribution: identifying the dominant knowledge source behind each output. We show that a probe, a simp

memory_insight.memory_bandwidth_requirements: up to 70% increase in error rates due to knowledge source confusion

analysis.engineering_takeaway: The use of AttriWiki data pipeline can significantly improve the accuracy of contributive attribution in LLMs, reducing error rates by up to 70%.
technical_details: Linear classifier trained on model hidden representations
Training on Llama-3.1-8B, Mistral-7B, and Qwen-7B models
Achieving up to 0.96 Macro-F1 on Llama-3.1-8B, Mistral-7B, and Qwen-7B
Transferring to out-of-domain benchmarks (SQuAD, WebQuestions) with 0.94-0.99 Macro-F1 without retraining
analysis.deployment_feasibility: Possible, but may require significant computational resources and memory. The actual bottleneck may be the model's computational requirements rather than memory usage.

review_questions: Is this genuinely a breakthrough? Or incremental improvement?
Is the DRAM impact claim supported by benchmarks?
